nativeRange.habitat: Marshes, swamps, and shallow margins of slow-moving streams and ponds; grows both submersed and emersed, often forming dense floating mats

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate preferred but tolerates plain gravel or sand
open background planting area with room to spread
best displayed in small groups of stems at the back of the tank
regular trimming required to prevent it overrunning the tank

avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as goldfish, silver dollars, scats, or large cichlids

summary: Spadeleaf (Gymnocoronis spilanthoides) is a fast-growing background stem plant from South America with bright, light-green spade-shaped leaves. Undemanding and beginner-friendly, but legally restricted in many regions due to its invasiveness.
careNotes: Spadeleaf is an undemanding, fast-growing background stem plant from tropical and southern South America, valued in planted tanks for its bright, light-green leaves and rapid nutrient uptake, which generally helps suppress algae. Substrate is flexible — plain gravel or sand is accepted, though nutrient-rich aquasoil with regular fertilization will produce fuller, more vigorous growth. Under typical aquarium conditions the plant reaches 30–60 cm, but in ponds or grown emersed (above the waterline) it has been reported to reach around 90 cm. Frequent trimming is essential — Spadeleaf grows extremely quickly and will rapidly overrun a tank if neglected. Light demand is generally considered medium, though the plant is capable of faster, lusher growth under higher light. IMPORTANT REGULATORY STATUS: This species is listed on the EU's Invasive Alien Species of Union Concern (Regulation 1143/2014, listed August 2019), making import, keeping, breeding, transport, sale, and propagation illegal across all EU member states and the UK without a special research permit. It is also a declared noxious or invasive weed in Australia (notifiable in several states), New Zealand (banned), Japan, and parts of the United States. Hobbyists in unrestricted jurisdictions should never release trimmings or unwanted plants into waterways — disposed material must be dried out or composted.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward via stem cuttings: trim a healthy side shoot or top section with at least two nodes and replant directly into the substrate, where it readily roots within a few weeks. The plant also produces small white flower heads when grown emersed and can be propagated by seed or division, but cuttings are by far the most common aquarium method. Regular trimming both controls the plant's size and yields propagation material — disposed trimmings in restricted regions should be dried out or composted, never flushed or released into local waterways.

primary.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
primary.notes: Primary source after Tropica fallback. Tropica's catalog page for Gymnocoronis spilanthoides returns an XSLT error (the plant has been delisted by Tropica following the August 2019 EU Invasive Alien Species ban). Flowgrow provided: family Asteraceae, order Asterales, native range tropical/southern South America, temperature 4-30°C (optimum 15-28°C), pH 5-8, kH 0-14 °dKH, light medium-high, CO2 10-40 mg/L, growth rate very fast, difficulty easy, background placement, propagation by cuttings.

additional.name: Aquatic Community
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Asteraceae), South American origin, height 30-60 cm, width 10-20 cm, temperature 15-30°C, pH 5.5-8, light medium to very high, growth rate very fast, propagation by side shoots/cuttings, difficulty easy, background placement.

additional.name: AquaInfo
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 10-30°C, fast growth, CO2-optional, easy propagation by cuttings (min 2 nodes) or seed/division. Confirmed common names 'Senegal Tea Plant' and 'Water Snowball'. Confirmed EU invasive listing 2019 with full keeping/breeding/sale/propagation prohibition without research permit.

additional.name: Florida Aquatic Nurseries
additional.notes: Cross-checked South American origin, large size (up to 3 ft / ~90 cm in pond/garden settings), medium light, very fast growth, beginner-friendly. US-based wholesale source confirming continued legal availability in the United States.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Asteraceae, Asterales, Eupatorieae tribe). Confirmed Senegal Tea Plant common name. Confirmed EU Invasive Alien Species listing since 2019 — import and trade forbidden EU-wide. First described by Augustin Pyramus de Candolle.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Banned across the EU and UK under Invasive Alien Species Regulation 1143/2014 (listed 2019); also restricted as an invasive noxious weed in Australia, New Zealand, and Japan. Sale, keeping, and propagation are illegal in these jurisdictions without a research permit. Check local regulations before acquiring.
